agreement.full_name: Global System of Trade Preferences among Developing Countries

tariff_sheets.header_row: REPEATING - see notes, NOT a single fixed row

notes: CONFIRMED BY DIRECT INSPECTION — this sheet does NOT have one fixed header_row like every other module. It is a VERTICAL, REPEATING-BLOCK structure: 45 country sections stacked in one sheet, each starting with a title row ('GLOBAL SYSTEM OF TRADE PREFERENCES - CONCESSIONS GRANTED BY <COUNTRY>'), followed immediately by its OWN 'Line_No | Country | <code-col> | <desc-col> | Base_Rate | GSTP Concession(*) Rate of Duty | Remarks' header row, then that country's data rows, then the next country's title/header/data block, and so on (confirmed for Algeria, Argentina, Bangladesh, Benin... through Zimbabwe — 45 blocks total). CRITICAL FURTHER FINDING: column C's header label ITSELF changes per country block — 'Tariff_Item_Number' for most, but 'Tariff Item Number HS' (India), 'HS Codification' (Morocco), 'Tariff Item Number CCCN' (Malaysia/Philippines/South Korea/Tanzania), 'NCM (Based on HS96)' (Paraguay), 'NANDINA (Product Code)' (Venezuela) — all semantically the same field (a pre-HS or country-specific product code, per Document B's PTN/GSTP historical-nomenclature note) but with inconsistent labels. Column D's header similarly varies ('Product_Description' vs 'Description of Product(1)' vs 'DESCRIPTION OF PRODUCT'). THIS MODULE CANNOT BE LOADED WITH THE STANDARD single-header_row CONFIG SHAPE used by every other file. It requires a DEDICATED PARSING APPROACH in its format handler: scan down the sheet, detect each new title row (matches pattern 'CONCESSIONS GRANTED BY'), treat the row immediately after as that block's header (column positions are consistent A-G even though labels vary), and read that block's country name from column B rather than from the config. Flagging requires_custom_parsing=true so the loader knows this file cannot use the generic simple column-letter approach. Per Document B, description is the PRIMARY reference for these historical pre-HS codes since the codes themselves are not standard HS — hs_code_norm should still be computed by HsCode::normalize() from column C for whatever digits exist, but product_desc (column D) is what search should weight most heavily for this module, per the documented design intent.
